description: A widely used over-the-counter analgesic and antipyretic — it eases mild-to-moderate pain and lowers fever. One of the most common household medicines; safe at normal doses but a leading cause of acute liver failure in overdose.

halfLife.conditions: healthy adults; mean (SD 0.6 h) after IV administration

vd.conditions: adults; volume of distribution at steady state, mean (SD 0.2 L/kg); scaled to absolute litres against the 70 kg reference subject at derivation time

metabolites.name: Paracetamol glucuronide
metabolites.description: The largest single product of paracetamol metabolism: the liver caps it with glucuronic acid to make an inactive, water-soluble conjugate the kidneys excrete. No painkilling and no toxicity — simply the main disposal route.

fractionFormed.conditions: About 55% of a paracetamol dose is glucuronidated in the liver (NCBI Bookshelf paracetamol monograph: '~55, 30, 4 and 4% of a therapeutic dose ... glucuronic acid, sulfuric acid, cysteine and mercapturic acid' — the classic healthy-adult urinary-recovery split; range across sources 47–62%). That 55% is a MOLAR fraction (fraction of molecules), but the engine's fm multiplies parent MASS cleared (dA_m/dt = fm·CL·C_p, no MW factor), so it is MW-adjusted: paracetamol glucuronide (MW 327.29) is heavier than paracetamol (151.16) — glucuronidation adds a glucuronic-acid group — so each mg of parent taking this path yields 327.29/151.16 = 2.165 mg of glucuronide: mass-fm = 0.55 × 2.165 = 1.191 = 119.1%. derived: true (molar figure MW-adjusted), the morphine/caffeine mass-fraction convention. The two conjugates' mass-fm sum (119.1 + 45.9 = 165%) exceeds 100% — expected and correct for metabolites heavier than the parent (the underlying molar fractions sum to 85% < 100%); not double-counting. Systemic (hepatic) formation on both IV paths; no first-pass (ffp) term because first pass is oral-only and this compound is modelled IV-only.

vd.conditions: ASSUMED volume of distribution, ~0.2 L/kg (14 L at 70 kg) — NOT measured. A conjugate the kidneys clear FASTER than the liver forms it (formation-rate-limited) does not reveal its own volume from parent-dosing plasma data; population PK models acknowledge this by FIXING the metabolite volume (e.g. to ~18% of the parent's central volume). Set here to extracellular-water order (~0.2 L/kg), consistent with these polar conjugates distributing in a volume 'considerably smaller than' the parent's ~0.8 L/kg. Load-bearing only for the PEAK HEIGHT of the line; the exposure (AUC_m = fm·Dose/CL_m) is independent of it. Paired with the glucuronide's renal clearance (Morris & Levy 1984) to derive the half-life. derived: true (assumed, not reported by the source).

halfLife.conditions: DERIVED, not observed: t½ = ln2·Vd/CL_m = 0.693 × 14 L / 6.41 L/h = 1.51 h. Vd is the assumed 0.2 L/kg (14 L); CL_m is the glucuronide's renal clearance, taken as its renal-clearance-to-creatinine ratio from Morris & Levy 1984 (0.890 ± 0.153; 8 healthy adults, 1.5 g dose) × a standard creatinine clearance of ~120 mL/min (7.2 L/h) = 6.4 L/h. Renal clearance is essentially the glucuronide's TOTAL elimination (it is polar and excreted unchanged, not further metabolised). The derived half-life is SHORTER than the parent's 2.4 h, which is the point: the glucuronide is FORMATION-rate-limited, so its plasma line rises and falls with the parent rather than showing its own faster elimination (terminal slope = min(k_parent, k_metabolite) = k_parent). This intrinsic ~1.5 h is deliberately stored INSTEAD of the long apparent conjugate half-lives (~15 h) quoted in renal-failure patients, where these renally-cleared conjugates accumulate — the intrinsic-over-apparent honesty rule (cf. the morphine glucuronides, where apparent > intrinsic for a different reason).

metabolites.notes:
Paracetamol glucuronide is the LARGEST single product of paracetamol metabolism (~55% of a dose; hepatic UDP-glucuronosyltransferase). It is pharmacologically inactive and NON-toxic — the main safe disposal route — so it is drawn dashed and tagged 'metabolite', not 'active metabolite'. It is the dominant conjugate LINE (mass-fm 119%, ~2.6× the sulfate by AUC), the plottable counterpoint to the reactive NAPQI pathway that is deliberately NOT plotted (see the compound metabolism prose and notes).

DISPOSITION — FORMATION-RATE-LIMITED (the teaching contrast with cefotaxime). fm and renal clearance are citable; the metabolite VOLUME is not identifiable from parent dosing, so it is assumed (~0.2 L/kg). Because the derived half-life (~1.5 h) is shorter than the parent's (2.4 h), the glucuronide is formation-rate-limited: its concentration tracks the parent (C_m ≈ (fm·CL_parent/CL_m)·C_parent) and its terminal slope is the parent's. This is the OPPOSITE of desacetylcefotaxime (elimination-rate-limited, a longer half-life than its parent). The AMOUNT is faithful — AUC_m = fm·Dose/CL_m is pinned by the cited fm and renal clearance, independent of the assumed volume; only the peak height carries the volume assumption.

metabolites.name: Paracetamol sulfate
metabolites.description: The second major metabolite: the liver caps paracetamol with a sulfate group to make an inactive, water-soluble conjugate the kidneys excrete. This route saturates at higher doses, shifting more of the drug to glucuronidation.

fractionFormed.conditions: About 30% of a paracetamol dose is sulfated in the liver (NCBI Bookshelf monograph; range 25–36%, and consistent with Clements, Critchley & Prescott 1984, who recovered 33–38% of the dose as sulfate in healthy adults after both oral and IV dosing). MOLAR fraction MW-adjusted to the engine's mass basis: paracetamol sulfate (MW 231.23) is heavier than paracetamol (151.16), so mass-fm = 0.30 × (231.23/151.16) = 0.30 × 1.530 = 0.459 = 45.9%. derived: true. Sulfation is the more readily SATURATED pathway (finite cofactor PAPS): its fractional contribution falls at higher doses as glucuronidation takes over — one source of acetaminophen's overdose nonlinearity, out of scope for this therapeutic-range linear model. Systemic formation, no ffp (see the glucuronide note and compound notes).

vd.conditions: ASSUMED volume of distribution, ~0.2 L/kg (14 L at 70 kg) — NOT measured, for the same reason as the glucuronide: a formation-rate-limited conjugate's volume cannot be identified from parent-dosing plasma data (population models fix it, e.g. to ~18% of the parent's central volume). Set to extracellular-water order for a polar conjugate. Load-bearing only for the line's PEAK HEIGHT; the exposure AUC_m = fm·Dose/CL_m is independent of it. Paired with the sulfate's renal clearance (Morris & Levy 1984) to derive the half-life. derived: true (assumed, not reported).

halfLife.conditions: DERIVED: t½ = ln2·Vd/CL_m = 0.693 × 14 L / 10.30 L/h = 0.94 h. CL_m is the sulfate's renal clearance = ratio 1.43 ± 0.250 (Morris & Levy 1984, 8 healthy adults) × ~120 mL/min creatinine clearance (7.2 L/h) = 10.3 L/h — HIGHER than the glucuronide's, because the sulfate undergoes active renal tubular SECRETION (its renal clearance exceeds glomerular filtration; the creatinine ratio > 1). Formation-rate-limited like the glucuronide (0.94 h ≪ parent 2.4 h), so its line tracks the parent. The sulfate's renal secretion is mildly SATURABLE (Morris & Levy: its renal clearance falls as its serum concentration rises), a small nonlinearity ignored in this therapeutic-range linear model. Intrinsic value stored, not the renal-failure apparent (~26 h).

metabolites.notes: Paracetamol sulfate is the SECOND major conjugate (~30% of a dose; hepatic sulfotransferase using the cofactor PAPS), inactive and non-toxic, cleared renally. Drawn as a dashed 'metabolite' line below the glucuronide (mass-fm 46% vs 119%). Two teaching points: (1) its renal clearance exceeds glomerular filtration (active tubular secretion; Morris & Levy renal-to-creatinine ratio 1.43 > 1), so it clears faster than the glucuronide; (2) sulfation is the readily SATURATED pathway (finite PAPS) — at higher/overdose doses its share falls and more drug is glucuronidated and oxidised, part of why acetaminophen turns nonlinear and more hepatotoxic in overdose (out of scope for this linear model). Like the glucuronide it is formation-rate-limited (derived t½ 0.94 h ≪ parent 2.4 h) so its line tracks the parent; AUC faithful via AUC_m = fm·Dose/CL_m, the peak height carrying the assumed volume.

fda_label.title: Acetaminophen Injection, Solution (Baxter Healthcare Corporation) — FDA Structured Product Label, clinical pharmacology (Table 5)

nbk526213.title: Paracetamol (Acetaminophen) — NCBI Bookshelf (NBK526213), metabolism section: about 55%, 30%, 4% and 4% of a therapeutic dose is excreted after hepatic conjugation with glucuronic acid, sulfuric acid, cysteine and mercapturic acid respectively; only 2–5% is excreted unchanged; ~8–10% is oxidised by CYP2E1 to the reactive NAPQI. Used for the metabolite formation fractions (fm).

morris_levy_1984.title: Morris ME, Levy G. Renal clearance and serum protein binding of acetaminophen and its major conjugates in humans. J Pharm Sci. 1984;73(8):1038–1041 (PMID 6491906). Eight healthy adults, 2 h after 1.5 g acetaminophen: renal clearance relative to creatinine clearance was 0.058 ± 0.026 (acetaminophen), 0.890 ± 0.153 (glucuronide) and 1.43 ± 0.250 (sulfate); the sulfate conjugate undergoes active renal tubular secretion (ratio > 1) that is saturable (renal clearance falls as serum conjugate rises); serum protein binding of the conjugates is low (<10%). Used for the conjugates' renal (≈ total) elimination clearances.

clements_prescott_1984.title: Clements JA, Critchley JAJH, Prescott LF. The role of sulphate conjugation in the metabolism and disposition of oral and intravenous paracetamol in man. Br J Clin Pharmacol. 1984;18(4):481–485 (PMID 6487489). Five healthy subjects, 5 and 20 mg/kg oral and IV: sulphate conjugate 37.7% (5 mg/kg) and 33.3% (20 mg/kg) of dose; sulphate renal clearance 273 and 205 mL/min; total paracetamol plasma clearance 331 and 295 mL/min; fractional urinary excretion of paracetamol and its conjugates independent of route; dose-corrected conjugate AUC ratios dose-independent (linearity across the therapeutic range). Used as a cross-check for the sulfate fraction and for linearity/route-independence.

metabolism: At normal doses acetaminophen is disposed of mainly by conjugation in the liver: about 55% of a dose becomes the glucuronide and about 30% the sulfate — both inactive, water-soluble products that the kidneys excrete. These two conjugates are drawn as dashed lines (the glucuronide the larger of the two). Given intravenously the parent starts high and only falls, while each conjugate first rises to a peak a couple of hours in as it forms; thereafter its terminal decline runs PARALLEL to the parent's, because the kidneys clear the conjugates faster than the liver forms them — they are 'formation-rate-limited', so their fall is paced by the parent rather than by their own faster elimination. A smaller fraction (~8–10%) is instead oxidised by the liver enzyme CYP2E1 to a reactive toxin, NAPQI, which is normally mopped up by glutathione — in overdose glutathione runs out and NAPQI injures the liver. NAPQI is too short-lived and reactive to appear in the blood, so — unlike the two conjugates — it cannot be plotted as a concentration curve: it is the dangerous metabolite you never see in plasma. The amounts of glucuronide and sulfate shown are faithful (fixed by their formation fractions and their renal clearance, the latter taken from measured renal-clearance ratios scaled to a standard kidney function); their exact peak heights depend on an assumed distribution volume, because a conjugate cleared faster than it is formed does not reveal its own volume from parent dosing alone.
notes:
Curated from the FDA Structured Product Label for intravenous acetaminophen (Baxter), adult clinical-pharmacology values. Disposition is route-independent, so both IV routes share it. Vd is reported as 0.8 (SD 0.2) L/kg and is scaled to absolute litres against the 70 kg illustrative reference subject during derivation. ke is taken from the elimination half-life (2.4 h), NOT from clearance: the label also reports systemic clearance 0.27 (SD 0.08) L/h/kg, which implies ke = CL/Vd = 0.27/0.8 ≈ 0.338 /h — about 17% higher than ke = ln2/t½ ≈ 0.289 /h. For a one-compartment model the two cannot both be exact; the half-life is used so the plotted terminal slope matches the stated 2.4 h, and the clearance value is recorded here for transparency rather than silently dropped. Plasma protein binding is low (10–25%). PK is dose-proportional across single 500/650/1000 mg doses (label), so linear superposition is valid at therapeutic doses; acetaminophen becomes nonlinear in overdose (saturable glucuronidation/sulfation, shunting to the toxic NAPQI pathway), a regime out of scope for this linear model. IV bolus is an instantaneous-input idealization; clinically the label dose is given as a ~15-minute infusion, so the iv_infusion route is the more realistic shape.

METABOLITES — GLUCURONIDE + SULFATE (added this pass; NAPQI stays in prose). Acetaminophen's two major conjugates are drawn as dashed lines off the one-compartment parent, exercising the systemic-formation metabolite path on both IV routes (no ffp — first pass is oral-only and this compound is IV-only). They are the FORMATION-rate-limited counterpoint to cefotaxime's elimination-rate-limited desacetylcefotaxime.

PARAMETERISATION (advisor-reviewed) — the honest split of citable vs assumed. (1) fm: molar urinary-recovery fractions (glucuronide 55%, sulfate 30%; NCBI Bookshelf paracetamol monograph, the sulfate cross-checked against Clements/Prescott 1984's 33–38%), MW-adjusted to the engine's mass basis (×327.29/151.16 = 2.165 and ×231.23/151.16 = 1.530 → 119.1% and 45.9%; the sum >100% is expected for heavier conjugates, molar sum 85%). (2) CL_m: the conjugates are eliminated essentially entirely by the kidney unchanged, so each metabolite's elimination clearance IS its renal clearance — taken from Morris & Levy 1984 (8 healthy adults) as the renal-clearance-to-creatinine ratios (glucuronide 0.890, sulfate 1.43) × a standard creatinine clearance ~120 mL/min = 6.4 and 10.3 L/h. (3) Vd_m: ASSUMED ~0.2 L/kg (extracellular water) — genuinely NOT identifiable from parent-dosing data for a formation-rate-limited metabolite (population models FIX it, e.g. to 18% of parent central volume). (4) t½_m = ln2·Vd/CL_m, DERIVED (glucuronide 1.51 h, sulfate 0.94 h).

WHY THIS IS HONEST — the Vd assumption CANCELS in the exposure. AUC_m = fm·Dose/CL_m depends only on the CITED fm and renal clearance, NOT on the assumed volume, so the metabolite EXPOSURE (the amount) is faithful; the assumed Vd shifts only the peak HEIGHT. This is the diazepam→nordiazepam posture (metabolite Vd derived, not measured), applied to a case where the volume is genuinely unidentifiable rather than merely unreported — spelt out plainly rather than dressed as a measurement (the lithium lesson).

THE FORMATION-LIMITED GATE (the check that made this shippable). Both derived half-lives come out SHORTER than the parent's 2.4 h, so both conjugates are formation-rate-limited: their plasma lines track the parent (C_m ≈ (fm·CL_parent/CL_m)·C_parent) and their terminal slope is the parent's. Had a derived t½ come out LONGER than the parent's, the metabolite would be elimination-rate-limited, the assumed volume would dominate the tail, and the line would NOT be shippable without a directly-measured metabolite disposition (the morphine-needs-direct-IV-conjugate situation) — so the gate was checked with the actual numbers before writing. The stored intrinsic half-lives are deliberately NOT the long APPARENT conjugate half-lives (~15–26 h) reported in renal-failure patients, where the renally-cleared conjugates accumulate.

MAGNITUDE CHECK (built engine curves). For a 1000 mg IV dose the modelled glucuronide plasma line towers over the parent (mass AUC ratio ~3.0×, molar ~1.4×) and the sulfate runs below it (mass AUC ~0.7× parent, molar ~0.5×), reproducing the observed dominance of the glucuronide among plasma conjugates; the modelled glucuronide:parent molar AUC ~1.4 sits at the conservative (low) end of the reported ~1.5–3, the same under-drawn direction as the morphine glucuronides. Both lines rise and fall with the parent (formation-limited), the intended teaching shape.

NAPQI — THE DANGEROUS METABOLITE YOU NEVER PLOT. The ~8–10% oxidative pathway (CYP2E1) forms NAPQI, the reactive intermediate responsible for hepatotoxicity in overdose. It is consumed at its site of formation (conjugated by hepatic glutathione to cysteine/mercapturate) and does not circulate — it has no citable plasma volume or half-life, so drawing it as a Bateman curve would require FABRICATED disposition. It is therefore kept in the metabolism prose (and named here) rather than plotted — itself a teaching point about why the most important metabolite toxicologically is invisible in plasma. Educational illustration only — not for dosing.
